attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Both kernels in single file `nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c`. Saving state, capturing baseline checkpoint, transitioning to analyze.

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash -c 'LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
# Persist select-step results
python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" <<PYEOF
import json,sys
f=sys.argv[1]
s=json.load(open(f))
s["source_file"] = "Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast.c"
s["cycles_before"] = "576115"
s["instrs_before"] = "392760"
s["active_path_file"] = "${CFG_WORK_DIR}/riscv_nn_conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast.i".replace("\${CFG_WORK_DIR}","/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/work")
s["called_functions"] = ["nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft_unroll4","nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft"]
s["called_function_files"] = ["Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c"]
json.dump(s,open(f,"w"),indent=2)
PYEOF
# Round-0 baseline checkpoint
FN="conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast"
REF="refs/optimize-ckpt/${FN}/round-0-baseline"
SHA=$(cd "${LIB_ROOT}" && git stash create "optimize ${FN} round-0 baseline" 2>/dev/null)
if [ -n "${SHA}" ]; then
  git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" "${SHA}"
  echo "baseline ref ${REF} -> stash blob ${SHA}"
else
  git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" HEAD
  echo "baseline ref ${REF} -> HEAD (no uncommitted changes)"
fi
python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "${REF}" <<PYEOF
import json,sys
f,ref=sys.argv[1],sys.argv[2]
s=json.load(open(f))
s["baseline_ref"]=ref
json.dump(s,open(f,"w"),indent=2)
PYEOF
# Transition select -> analyze
python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "analyze" "${SCRIPT_DIR}" <<PYEOF
import json,os,sys,subprocess
from datetime import datetime
f,new_step=sys.argv[1],sys.argv[2]
script_dir=sys.argv[3]
s=json.load(open(f))
prev_step=s.get("step","select")
now=datetime.now()
prev_start=datetime.fromisoformat(s.get("prev_step_started",now.isoformat()))
elapsed=(now-prev_start).total_seconds()
s["last_step_duration"]=f"{prev_step}: {elapsed:.1f}s"
s["step"]=new_step
s["step_started"]=now.isoformat()
s["history"]=s.get("history",[])+[f"{now.isoformat()} {new_step}"]
s["prev_step_started"]=s["step_started"]
json.dump(s,open(f,"w"),indent=2)
PYEOF
cat "${CFG_STATE_FILE}"'

[effort: xhigh]

You are the **analyze proposer** in the libnn /optimize FSM, round 1 for `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast`.

## Goal
Identify the highest-leverage **micro-architecture tuning** opportunity (instruction scheduling/interleaving, SW-pipelining, peephole optimization, VRF port-conflict resolution, dual-issue constraints) for the AX45MPV V1024_8MB target. Focus on the inner loop of the kernel that dominates cycles.

## Context (already done by main agent)

# Analyze round 1: conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast

## Active code path
- conv: `Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast.c:51-285` -- `ENA_VEC_ISA && ENA_TILING` branch. im2col into 4-pixel batches, calls `nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft_unroll4` (lines 90, 129, 154, 189, 227 -- 5 sites) and a single `nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft` for 2-pixel drain (line 250). Perf binary baseline confirms 5 unroll4 calls + 1 tail.
- hot kernel: `Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c:36-235` -- `ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD && ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` branch (line 36). Inner reduction loop is lines 86-137: 8 vle8.v at LMUL=m1 (4 weight rows pA..pA4 + 4 input cols pB..pB4), then a vsetvli to e32m1 (vl/=4) and 16 vd4dotsu.vv into vAcc0..vAcc15.

## Inner-loop assembly summary
From `libnn.objdump:118266-118303` (label `.L4`), per iteration:
- LMUL/EMUL: e8 m1 for vle8 (16 cyc total at 2 cyc/op throughput); e32 m1 for vd4dotsu (16 cyc total at 1 cyc/op).
- Register groups in use: **v0-v3** (pA..pA4 loads), **v4-v7** (pB..pB4 loads), **v8-v23** (16 i32 accumulators), **v31** (zero for redsum). v24-v30 are FREE (7 registers).
- Per-iter instructions: 2 vsetvli + 8 vle8 + 8 scalar add + 1 srli + 16 vd4dotsu + 1 sub + 1 bnez = ~37 dynamic instrs.
- **Bottleneck**: VLSU and VMAC are NOT overlapped. The 16 vd4dotsu reads v0-v7 (results of the 8 vle8) -- a strict RAW chain. Steady-state cycles = 16 (VLSU) + 16 (VMAC) = ~32 cyc/iter. With perfect pipelining (Phase A loads next batch into spare regs while Phase B vd4dotsu computes on the loaded batch) the steady-state drops to **max(VLSU=16, VMAC=16) = 16 cyc/iter** (VMAC + VLSU is a clean dual-issue pair per the V1024_8MB rules, with VLSU as 2nd slot).
- Secondary: the post-reduction tail (vredsum chain at 118307, slideup chain at 118324, requantize chain at 118337-118369) has 16 vredsum (e32m1: 9+1 = 10 cyc latency each, but they hit different dst regs so they pipeline; mostly throughput-bound at LMUL=1) and a long alternating vmax/vnsra/vnclipu chain, similar to what the s8 round-6 batched-clamp peephole exploited.

## Metrics
For one inner-loop iteration at VLMAX (vl=128 e8):
- Loads: 8 vle8.v * 128 B = **1024 B / iter**.
- Compute: 16 vd4dotsu.vv each producing 128 elements x 4 i8 MACs = 512 MACs = 1024 ops/elem... per iter = **16 * 128 * 4 * 2 = 16384 op (8192 MACs)**.
- Arithmetic intensity = 8192 MACs / 1024 B = **8 MACs/byte loaded** = 16 ops/byte. Reuse rate is high (4 weight rows * 4 input cols = 16x reuse on each loaded vector).
- Bandwidth need at 16 cyc/iter: 1024 B / 16 cyc = 64 B/cyc -- matches L2 peak EXACTLY but D$ should hit (working set per call: 4 cols * K=288 B input + out_ch=128 * K=288 B weights = 1.1KB + 36KB. Weights miss L2-line repeatedly across 5 calls but stream linearly; D$ prefetcher should keep up, and the 16-entry VLSU MSHR has slack).
- Bound: compute/dual-issue (VMAC throughput == VLSU throughput at m1, with no overlap currently). Memory is sufficient. **Latency-hiding bound** = inner loop is NOT pipelined.

## Wiki insights applied
- `ax45mpv-v1024-8mb-pipeline-tuning.md`: vd4dots m1 = 1 cyc; vle8 m1 = 2*EMUL = 2 cyc; clean dual-issue pair `VMAC + VLSU` with VLSU as 2nd slot.
- `anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group.md`: not violated -- this kernel uses LMUL=m1 (32 reg groups), so SW pipelining HAS spare register groups (v24-v30 are unused, and the load buffers v0-v7 can be relocated). The anti-pattern triggers only when LMUL=m8 leaves only 4 groups all consumed.
- Recent learning (commit be791370 on s8 sibling `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4`): **identical structural pattern** (4x4 vd4dots tile, accumulators in v0-v15, load buffers split v16-v23/v24-v31, alternating Phase A/B). Achieved 1.0716x cumulative on a similar baseline. The u8 kernel here has the SAME structure but accumulators are placed in v8-v23 instead of v0-v15, so a register-block remap is needed first to free v16-v31 for the alternating buffers.

## Ranked hypotheses

### H1 (top pick): SW-pipeline the unroll4 inner vd4dotsu loop with alternating load buffers
- **What to change**: `Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c:36-235` -- the `ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD && ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` branch. Specifically:
  1. **Structural remap (Tidy First)**: relocate the 16 i32m1 accumulators from v8-v23 to **v0-v15** (line-by-line: change `vAcc0..vAcc15` register pressure -- when written via intrinsics, the compiler does the placement, but we control it via the asm-style sibling kernel pattern). Since this branch uses intrinsics, we should mirror the s8 commit's approach: switch to NDS_VEC_* macro form (`#elif defined(ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD)` block at lines 381-561) OR apply explicit register hints. The s8 sibling chose explicit `NDS_VEC_*` macros for full register control -- recommended to match. The accumulators land in v0-v15, V31 stays as redsum-zero, V20-V23 / V24-V31 alternate as pA0-3/pB0-3 buffers (8 regs per phase).
  2. **SW-pipeline body** (lines 86-137 conceptually; physical lines after macro-form switch): peel first iteration to load Phase-A buffers (v16-v23: pA0-3 + pB0-3), then alternate per iteration:
     - Phase A: vsetvli e8m1; vle8 8x into v24-v31 (next iter's data); vsetvli e32m1 (vl_pending); 16 vd4dots reading v16-v23 (this iter's data) into v0-v15.
     - Phase B: symmetric, swap v16-v23 <-> v24-v31.
  3. **Drain**: final 16 vd4dots on the last-loaded buffer with no overlapping load. Mirror s8 lines 1899-1938.
- **Expected impact**: per-iter ~32 cyc -> ~16-18 cyc steady state on the VMAC-VLSU pipelined section. Inner loop runs 3 iters per (rowCnt, colCnt) sub-call. Total inner-iter count for the perf shape (K=288, out_ch=128 -> rowCnt=32, 5 calls): 5 * 32 * 3 = 480 iters; savings ~14 cyc/iter * 480 = ~6720 cyc just from main-loop iters. But the inner loop is the dominant share -- the s8 sibling at similar structure measured **+5-7%** on the function-level FPGA cycle count. Projected: **~30,000-40,000 cyc reduction, ~5-7% function-level speedup** (576115 -> ~535-545K).
- **Risk**: medium. Mitigations:
  - Register clobbering must be exact to avoid VRF port conflicts and to keep v31 (redsum zero) untouched -- s8 pattern already proves it works.
  - The peeled prologue + alternating-phase body adds I-cache pressure; AX45MPV I$=32KB/2-way -- the s8 kernel at ~240 lines did not blow I$. Same body size expected here.
  - Tail (`if(out_tensor_ch & 0x2)`) m2 path remains untouched.
  - Correctness validated by 3-call test; conv_HWC_u8 has the same im2col harness so the test exercises all phases.
- **Why this beats IPC 0.68**: current loop serializes VLSU and VMAC entirely. IPC 0.68 reflects ~32 cyc spent per ~22 vector instrs (16 vd4dots + 8 vle + ~3 setup) = 0.69 IPC. After pipelining, same instructions fit into ~16 cyc -> IPC ~1.4 (dual-issue VMAC+VLSU effective). The dual-issue pair `VMAC + VLSU (2nd slot)` is explicitly safe per the wiki. Spare register groups (v24-v30) are AVAILABLE since LMUL=m1 (this is the m1 case the anti-pattern wiki explicitly endorses for SW pipelining).

### H2: Batch the requantize tail (vmax then vmul/vsra/vadd then vnsra then vnclipu reordering)
- **What to change**: `Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c` lines 183-228 (intrinsic branch) and 513-548 (asm branch). Currently the requantize sequence interleaves operations across vSum0/vSum4/vSum8/vSum12 (already batched to 4 ops per stage -- this is good). However the per-stage ordering still mixes vsra/vmul/vadd/vmax sequentially per accumulator group; the s8 round-6 win was specifically batching vmax then vnsra (4-instr RAW gap hides VALU 2-3 cyc latency). Currently u8 already does `vmax x4` then `vnsra x4` (lines 205-211), so this is **already applied** at the structural level.
- **Expected impact**: marginal (<0.5%). Most of the 4-wide batching is already there.
- **Risk**: low.
- **Why**: low headroom; main win was H1 in the s8 sibling.

### H3: Hoist v31 zero-init out of the function (per commit d7a65489 on the s8 sibling)
- **What to change**: line 45-46 (intrinsic) and 395-396 (asm) -- `vl=vsetvli(1); vand v31,v31,0`. This is done per-call. Hoisting it to be set once per **conv-level invocation** (across all 5+1 unroll4/tail calls) saves ~2-3 cyc * 6 = ~18 cyc per conv -- negligible at function level. Already was a small win for s8 unroll4 because s8 was called many more times.
- **Expected impact**: <0.05%. Not worth pursuing in round 1.
- **Risk**: low but ABI-changing (v31 must be live across calls; conflicts with calling-convention assumptions).

## Pick for round 1
**H1**. The u8 unroll4 kernel has the same shape as the s8 unroll4 kernel that gained +7.16% via SW-pipeline, the LMUL=m1 setting leaves spare register groups (v24-v30 free; load buffers can be relocated), and the inner loop is the dominant cycle share. Apply the proven Phase-A/Phase-B alternating-buffer pattern from `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset.c:1820-1937`, with accumulators remapped to v0-v15 and load buffers alternating v16-v23 / v24-v31. Switching to the NDS_VEC_* macro form (mirroring the existing `#elif defined(ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD)` block at lines 381-561 of this file) gives exact register control and matches the s8 commit's implementation strategy.

- Per-iter instructions: 2 vsetvli + 8 vle8 + 8 scalar add + 1 srli + 16 vd4dotsu + 1 sub + 1 bnez = ~37 dynamic instrs.
- **Bottleneck**: VLSU and VMAC are NOT overlapped. The 16 vd4dotsu reads v0-v7 (results of the 8 vle8) -- a strict RAW chain. Steady-state cycles = 16 (VLSU) + 16 (VMAC) = ~32 cyc/iter. With perfect pipelining (Phase A loads next batch into spare regs while Phase B vd4dotsu computes on the loaded batch) the steady-state drops to **max(VLSU=16, VMAC=16) = 16 cyc/iter** (VMAC + VLSU is a clean dual-issue pair per the V1024_8MB rules, with VLSU as 2nd slot).
- Secondary: the post-reduction tail (vredsum chain at 118307, slideup chain at 118324, requantize chain at 118337-118369) has 16 vredsum (e32m1: 9+1 = 10 cyc latency each, but they hit different dst regs so they pipeline; mostly throughput-bound at LMUL=1) and a long alternating vmax/vnsra/vnclipu chain, similar to what the s8 round-6 batched-clamp peephole exploited.

## Metrics
For one inner-loop iteration at VLMAX (vl=128 e8):
- Loads: 8 vle8.v * 128 B = **1024 B / iter**.
- Compute: 16 vd4dotsu.vv each producing 128 elements x 4 i8 MACs = 512 MACs = 1024 ops/elem... per iter = **16 * 128 * 4 * 2 = 16384 op (8192 MACs)**.
- Arithmetic intensity = 8192 MACs / 1024 B = **8 MACs/byte loaded** = 16 ops/byte. Reuse rate is high (4 weight rows * 4 input cols = 16x reuse on each loaded vector).
- Bandwidth need at 16 cyc/iter: 1024 B / 16 cyc = 64 B/cyc -- matches L2 peak EXACTLY but D$ should hit (working set per call: 4 cols * K=288 B input + out_ch=128 * K=288 B weights = 1.1KB + 36KB. Weights miss L2-line repeatedly across 5 calls but stream linearly; D$ prefetcher should keep up, and the 16-entry VLSU MSHR has slack).
- Bound: compute/dual-issue (VMAC throughput == VLSU throughput at m1, with no overlap currently). Memory is sufficient. **Latency-hiding bound** = inner loop is NOT pipelined.

## Wiki insights applied
- `ax45mpv-v1024-8mb-pipeline-tuning.md`: vd4dots m1 = 1 cyc; vle8 m1 = 2*EMUL = 2 cyc; clean dual-issue pair `VMAC + VLSU` with VLSU as 2nd slot.
- `anti-sw-pipeline-same-reg-group.md`: not violated -- this kernel uses LMUL=m1 (32 reg groups), so SW pipelining HAS spare register groups (v24-v30 are unused, and the load buffers v0-v7 can be relocated). The anti-pattern triggers only when LMUL=m8 leaves only 4 groups all consumed.
- Recent learning (commit be791370 on s8 sibling `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4`): **identical structural pattern** (4x4 vd4dots tile, accumulators in v0-v15, load buffers split v16-v23/v24-v31, alternating Phase A/B). Achieved 1.0716x cumulative on a similar baseline. The u8 kernel here has the SAME structure but accumulators are placed in v8-v23 instead of v0-v15, so a register-block remap is needed first to free v16-v31 for the alternating buffers.

## Ranked hypotheses

### H1 (top pick): SW-pipeline the unroll4 inner vd4dotsu loop with alternating load buffers
- **What to change**: `Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c:36-235` -- the `ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD && ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` branch. Specifically:
  1. **Structural remap (Tidy First)**: relocate the 16 i32m1 accumulators from v8-v23 to **v0-v15** (line-by-line: change `vAcc0..vAcc15` register pressure -- when written via intrinsics, the compiler does the placement, but we control it via the asm-style sibling kernel pattern). Since this branch uses intrinsics, we should mirror the s8 commit's approach: switch to NDS_VEC_* macro form (`#elif defined(ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD)` block at lines 381-561) OR apply explicit register hints. The s8 sibling chose explicit `NDS_VEC_*` macros for full register control -- recommended to match. The accumulators land in v0-v15, V31 stays as redsum-zero, V20-V23 / V24-V31 alternate as pA0-3/pB0-3 buffers (8 regs per phase).
  2. **SW-pipeline body** (lines 86-137 conceptually; physical lines after macro-form switch): peel first iteration to load Phase-A buffers (v16-v23: pA0-3 + pB0-3), then alternate per iteration:
     - Phase A: vsetvli e8m1; vle8 8x into v24-v31 (next iter's data); vsetvli e32m1 (vl_pending); 16 vd4dots reading v16-v23 (this iter's data) into v0-v15.
     - Phase B: symmetric, swap v16-v23 <-> v24-v31.
  3. **Drain**: final 16 vd4dots on the last-loaded buffer with no overlapping load. Mirror s8 lines 1899-1938.
- **Expected impact**: per-iter ~32 cyc -> ~16-18 cyc steady state on the VMAC-VLSU pipelined section. Inner loop runs 3 iters per (rowCnt, colCnt) sub-call. Total inner-iter count for the perf shape (K=288, out_ch=128 -> rowCnt=32, 5 calls): 5 * 32 * 3 = 480 iters; savings ~14 cyc/iter * 480 = ~6720 cyc just from main-loop iters. But the inner loop is the dominant share -- the s8 sibling at similar structure measured **+5-7%** on the function-level FPGA cycle count. Projected: **~30,000-40,000 cyc reduction, ~5-7% function-level speedup** (576115 -> ~535-545K).
- **Risk**: medium. Mitigations:
  - Register clobbering must be exact to avoid VRF port conflicts and to keep v31 (redsum zero) untouched -- s8 pattern already proves it works.
  - The peeled prologue + alternating-phase body adds I-cache pressure; AX45MPV I$=32KB/2-way -- the s8 kernel at ~240 lines did not blow I$. Same body size expected here.
  - Tail (`if(out_tensor_ch & 0x2)`) m2 path remains untouched.
  - Correctness validated by 3-call test; conv_HWC_u8 has the same im2col harness so the test exercises all phases.
- **Why this beats IPC 0.68**: current loop serializes VLSU and VMAC entirely. IPC 0.68 reflects ~32 cyc spent per ~22 vector instrs (16 vd4dots + 8 vle + ~3 setup) = 0.69 IPC. After pipelining, same instructions fit into ~16 cyc -> IPC ~1.4 (dual-issue VMAC+VLSU effective). The dual-issue pair `VMAC + VLSU (2nd slot)` is explicitly safe per the wiki. Spare register groups (v24-v30) are AVAILABLE since LMUL=m1 (this is the m1 case the anti-pattern wiki explicitly endorses for SW pipelining).

### H2: Batch the requantize tail (vmax then vmul/vsra/vadd then vnsra then vnclipu reordering)
- **What to change**: `Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c` lines 183-228 (intrinsic branch) and 513-548 (asm branch). Currently the requantize sequence interleaves operations across vSum0/vSum4/vSum8/vSum12 (already batched to 4 ops per stage -- this is good). However the per-stage ordering still mixes vsra/vmul/vadd/vmax sequentially per accumulator group; the s8 round-6 win was specifically batching vmax then vnsra (4-instr RAW gap hides VALU 2-3 cyc latency). Currently u8 already does `vmax x4` then `vnsra x4` (lines 205-211), so this is **already applied** at the structural level.
- **Expected impact**: marginal (<0.5%). Most of the 4-wide batching is already there.
- **Risk**: low.
- **Why**: low headroom; main win was H1 in the s8 sibling.

### H3: Hoist v31 zero-init out of the function (per commit d7a65489 on the s8 sibling)
- **What to change**: line 45-46 (intrinsic) and 395-396 (asm) -- `vl=vsetvli(1); vand v31,v31,0`. This is done per-call. Hoisting it to be set once per **conv-level invocation** (across all 5+1 unroll4/tail calls) saves ~2-3 cyc * 6 = ~18 cyc per conv -- negligible at function level. Already was a small win for s8 unroll4 because s8 was called many more times.
- **Expected impact**: <0.05%. Not worth pursuing in round 1.
- **Risk**: low but ABI-changing (v31 must be live across calls; conflicts with calling-convention assumptions).

## Pick for round 1
**H1**. The u8 unroll4 kernel has the same shape as the s8 unroll4 kernel that gained +7.16% via SW-pipeline, the LMUL=m1 setting leaves spare register groups (v24-v30 free; load buffers can be relocated), and the inner loop is the dominant cycle share. Apply the proven Phase-A/Phase-B alternating-buffer pattern from `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset.c:1820-1937`, with accumulators remapped to v0-v15 and load buffers alternating v16-v23 / v24-v31. Switching to the NDS_VEC_* macro form (mirroring the existing `#elif defined(ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD)` block at lines 381-561 of this file) gives exact register control and matches the s8 commit's implementation strategy.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

      loads driving the requantize tail.

      alternating buffers V16-V23 / V24-V31, dual-issuing vle8 (Phase B loads)
      with 16 vd4dots (Phase A computes on prev-iter's V24-V31). Steady-state
      cycles/phase = max(VLSU, VMAC) = 16 cyc instead of serial 32 cyc.
    - r6: two peepholes -- batch all vmax then all vmin in the 4-channel
      requantize tail (4-instr RAW gap hides ~3-4 cyc VALU latency vs the 1-instr
      gap of the alternating order); merge the vsetvli vl=1 used to zero V31 into
      the vsetvli vl=num_col_a covering the vredsum chain (vand at e32 m1 is 1 cyc

input.description: Implement SW-pipeline in u8 unroll4 kernel

content.text:
The `pA = pA4` is at line 673, correctly positioned after both branches of the `if/else`. Everything looks structurally correct.

**Summary of changes (lines 395-774 in the new file, replacing original 395-561):**

- **Accumulator layout changed**: from V8-V23 (4 pix x 4 ch, pix-major) to V0-V15 (4 ch x 4 pix, ch-major), matching s8 sibling layout and enabling V16-V31 as SW-pipeline load buffers.
- **SW-pipeline prologue added**: when `colCnt >= maxvl_per_loop`, batch 0 is loaded into V16-V23 with V0-V15 zeroing interleaved; alternating-buffer loop (Phase A: compute V16-V23, load V24-V31; Phase B: compute V24-V31, load V16-V23) overlaps VLSU with VMAC.
- **Small-K fallback added**: when `colCnt < maxvl_per_loop`, falls back to a conventional zero-then-compute loop accumulating into V0-V15 with V16-V23 as load temporaries.
- **Tail handler added inside SW-pipeline path**: for remainder elements not multiple of VLMAX, zeros pixel buffers V20-V23 to 4-byte-rounded length then loads and computes the partial group.
- **Reduction epilogue rewritten**: 16 `VREDSUM` from V0-V15 into V16-V30+V0 (per-pixel channel sums), `vslideup` packs into V16/V20/V24/V28, V31 re-zeroed before vredsum since Phase A loads pB4 into V31, bias loaded into V1 (free register), requantize operates on V16/V20/V24/V28, stores via `VSB_V` to out/pOut2/pOut3/pOut4.
- **No changes** to lines 1-380 (ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC branch), lines 776+ (M2 tail + epilogue + other branches).

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: QEMU verify PASS (3 sub-tests). Transitioning to measure on FPGA.

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash -c 'LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "measure" "${SCRIPT_DIR}" <<PYEOF
import json,sys
from datetime import datetime
f,new_step,sd=sys.argv[1],sys.argv[2],sys.argv[3]
s=json.load(open(f))
prev_step=s.get("step","verify")
now=datetime.now()
prev_start=datetime.fromisoformat(s.get("prev_step_started",now.isoformat()))
elapsed=(now-prev_start).total_seconds()
s["last_step_duration"]=f"{prev_step}: {elapsed:.1f}s"
s["step"]=new_step
s["step_started"]=now.isoformat()
s["history"]=s.get("history",[])+[f"{now.isoformat()} {new_step}"]
s["prev_step_started"]=s["step_started"]
json.dump(s,open(f,"w"),indent=2)
PYEOF
/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh" conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast 2>&1 | tail -20'

content.text: REGRESSION: 576115 -> 592388 cyc (-2.83%, speedup 0.9725x). Auto-reverting and recording lesson.

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash -c 'LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
# Save measurement + record round_history
python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" <<PYEOF
import json,sys
f=sys.argv[1]
s=json.load(open(f))
s["cycles_after"]="592388"
s["instrs_after"]="398660"
s["speedup"]="0.9725"
s["round_history"]=[{
  "round":1,
  "approach":"SW-pipeline unroll4 vd4dotsu w/ alternating buffers V16-V23/V24-V31, accs remapped V8-V23->V0-V15 (mirror s8 sibling pattern)",
  "cycles":"592388",
  "instrs":"398660",
  "ipc":"0.67",
  "speedup":"0.9725",
  "outcome":"regressed",
  "insight":"Prologue+drain+tail-handler overhead exceeds main-loop savings when inner reduction has only ~2-3 iters (K=288, vl=128). +5900 instrs (+1.5%) with no IPC gain. s8 sibling won because its perf test had more inner iters; u8 perf test K is too small to amortize SW-pipeline scaffolding."
}]
json.dump(s,open(f,"w"),indent=2)
PYEOF
# Auto-revert
REF=$(python3 -c "import json;print(json.load(open(\"${CFG_STATE_FILE}\"))[\"checkpoint_ref\"])")
FILES=$(python3 -c "import json;print(\" \".join(json.load(open(\"${CFG_STATE_FILE}\")).get(\"changed_files\",[])))")
echo "reverting ${FILES} from ${REF}"
git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" checkout "${REF}" -- ${FILES}
/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh" ${FILES} 2>&1 | tail -3
# Sanity-check baseline restored
echo "--- sanity-check restored baseline ---"
/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_qemu_test.sh" conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast 2>&1 | tail -3'

TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.

in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,

Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.

input.content:
---
name: SW-pipeline amortization threshold for vd4dots tile kernels
description: When porting the s8 4x4 vd4dots SW-pipeline pattern to a sibling kernel (u8/etc.), check inner-iteration count first; <=3 iters per inner reduction call won't amortize the prologue+drain+tail-handler overhead.
type: feedback
---

For the proven 4x4 vd4dots SW-pipeline pattern (alternating buffers V16-V23/V24-V31, accs in V0-V15), the prologue+drain+tail-handler adds ~30-40 instrs per (rowCnt) iteration. To net positive on FPGA cycles, the inner reduction loop needs roughly **>=4-5 vl-sized iterations per call**.

**Why:** Steady-state savings are ~(VLSU+VMAC)/2 = ~16 cyc/iter. Scaffolding overhead is ~50-80 cyc/call. Break-even at ~4-5 iters; below that the rewrite regresses.

**How to apply:**
- Before porting the s8 round-5 SW-pipeline pattern (commit be791370 on `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset.c`) to any sibling kernel, compute `K / VLMAX_e8_m1` for the perf-test shape: `K = in_tensor_ch * ker_dim^2`, VLMAX = 128 at VLEN=1024.
- Example: u8 `conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast` perf shape K=288 -> 288/128 = 2-3 inner iters per call. Round-1 attempt (2026-04-28) regressed -2.83% (576115 -> 592388 cyc, +5900 instrs) confirming the threshold.
- Example: s8 `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any` perf shape gave +7.16% over rounds 1-6 because its perf test had more iterations.
- For low-K kernels, prefer cheaper peepholes (vsetvli barrier merging, batched vmax/vmin chains in requantize tail) instead of wholesale SW-pipeline rewrite.

**Detection signal:** If a SW-pipeline rewrite that QEMU-passes also INCREASES instruction count by more than ~1% on FPGA, the prologue/drain overhead is dominating -- revert and try a smaller-scope peephole.

1	# Memory Index
2	
3	- [AX45MPV vd4dots throughput](hw_ax45mpv_vd4dots_throughput.md) — vd4dots issues at 1/cyc; VW tail is latency, not throughput.
4	- [Pipeline-tuning predictions](feedback_pipeline_tuning_predictions.md) — V1024_8MB tuning page predicts inner-loop ceiling, not function ceiling; weight by inner-loop cycle share.
5	- [LMUL irrelevant for partial VL](feedback_lmul_partial_vl_throughput.md) — V1024_8MB VLSU 2*EMUL formula is worst-case-VL only; for vl<VLMAX_m1 m1==m4==m8 in cycles.
6	- [vle width unification breaks on int8 buffers](feedback_vle_alignment_unify_vsetvli.md) — vle8->vle32 to unify vsetvli for dual-issue fails: caller int8_t* not 4-byte aligned; QEMU hangs on misaligned vle32.
7	- [Clamp chains: batch vmax then vmin](feedback_clamp_chain_batching.md) — In requantize tails, batch all vmax then all vmin (not alternating) to hide VALU latency; measured +0.64% in unroll4 round 6.
8	- [llvm-mca for AX45MPV](reference_llvm_mca_ax45v.md) — Invocation flags for nx45v + Andes vdot/vqmac; what the model gets right vs wrong vs FPGA ground truth.

content.text:
Function #2 done. ROADMAP updated.

**Summary for `conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast`:**
- Baseline: **576,115 cyc** (IPC 0.68, 392,760 instrs)
- Round 1 (SW-pipeline mirror of s8 sibling pattern): **592,388 cyc** (-2.83%, REGRESSED) -> auto-reverted
- Final uarch_Opt: **576,115 cyc (1.00x)**
- Lesson saved: SW-pipeline scaffolding (prologue/drain/tail) requires >=4-5 inner iters per call to amortize. This perf shape K=288 / VLMAX=128 only gives 2-3 iters, so the rewrite added 5,900 instrs without IPC gain.

- Lesson: perf-test K=288 / VLMAX=128 = ~3 inner iters/call -- below the ~4-5 break-even for amortizing prologue+drain+tail-handler scaffolding. **Do NOT propose any approach that adds a prologue/drain/tail-handler scaffolding.**

## Constraints for round 2
1. **NO structural rewrite** that adds scaffolding (prologue / drain / separate tail handler). Keep the same loop structure: `while(rowCnt > 0) { while(colCnt > 0) {...8 vle + 16 vd4dotsu...} ...vredsum ...slideup ...requantize ...store }`.
2. **Total instruction count must NOT grow significantly** (>=0 cycles savings only if instr count holds or shrinks).
3. Prefer **peephole / scheduling / vsetvli-merge** changes that touch <30 lines and add 0-2 instrs.
4. Read the wiki and uarch refs FIRST. Look for techniques specifically applicable to this code shape:
   - `docs/wiki/general/ax45mpv-v1024-8mb-pipeline-tuning.md`
   - `docs/wiki/general/ax45mpv-vpu-uarch-spec.md`

   - `lmul_partial_vl_throughput` -- VLSU EMUL formula is worst-case-VL only.

Lines 564-685 is the `&0x2` tail (do not propose to modify; tail is rare-path).
- **Caller**: `Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast.c` -- check what the conv-side overhead looks like (5 unroll4 calls + 1 tail call). Also examine what the CONV LOOP itself does between mat-mul calls -- is there im2col packing that could be optimized?
- **Assembly**: `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/libnn.objdump` (regenerated after revert)

- **Kernel preprocessed**: `/home/nick/work/libnn_0421/.claude/skills/optimize/work/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.i` lines 1964-2107

## Baseline numbers
- 576115 cycles, 392760 instrs, IPC 0.68
- Per-iter inner: ~32 cyc (16 cyc VLSU + 16 cyc VMAC, serialized by RAW)
- Per-iter inner: ~37 instrs (2 vsetvli + 8 vle8 + 8 scalar-add + 1 srli + 16 vd4dotsu + 1 sub + 1 bnez)

## Specific candidate areas to look at carefully (give each a yes/no with rationale)

a. **Conv-side wins**: examine `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast.c`. The 5 unroll4 calls happen inside a loop. Is there an im2col packing step before each call that can be hoisted, parallelized, or otherwise tightened? What's the call overhead (function prologue/epilogue) per mat-mul invocation -- are caller-saved register-spill/reload chains visible in the perf objdump?

b. **vsetvli barrier elimination**: the inner loop has 2 vsetvli per iter (e8 m1 before vle, then e32 m1 with vl/=4 before vd4dotsu). Can these be merged or hoisted?

c. **Pointer-bump packing**: 8 scalar `pX += vl` after the vle8 loads. Can 4 of them be hoisted into a constant `step = vl` and use `pA += step` patterns? gcc may already do this; check the .objdump for actual instructions.

d. **vsetvli fix-issue rules from the V1024_8MB tuning doc**: are there specific dual-issue pairs we're missing? E.g., scalar instructions can dual-issue with VLSU/VMAC -- can we move scalar pointer-bumps to overlap with vle8?

e. **Reordering vd4dotsu**: 16 vd4dotsu read v0-v3 (weights) and v4-v7 (data). Currently arranged as 4 groups of 4 (one per pB row). Does the AX45MPV pipeline have a VRF read-port conflict for two consecutive vd4dotsu reading the SAME weight register? Check the uarch doc.

f. **V31 hoist (round 6 of s8 commit had a minor variant)**: V31 zero-init is ONCE per call (line 396). Already hoisted. So no further hoist available here -- mark this as N/A.

g. **Requantize tail batched-clamp**: already at lines 535-548 -- 4x vmax then vsetvli e16 then 4x vnsra then vsetvli e8 then 4x vnclipu. The vsetvli barriers between vmax and vnsra create RAW hazards. Can the vsetvli e16/e8 be MERGED with adjacent ops the way s8 commit did (vsetvli vl=num_col_a covering vredsum chain + vand v31)?

```

But try hard first -- there are usually small wins available in vsetvli merging, pointer-bump scheduling, or requantize chain reordering.

content.text:
Now an interesting opportunity: **bias load + ptr bump can happen earlier**. Currently `vle32 V30, bias` is at line 509, AFTER 12 vslideup. The vslideup chain is on VPERMUT FU. The vle32 is on VLSU (different FU). Can the vle32 run in parallel? YES — it's already after the slideups, but its data dep is only the vsetvli from line 491 `VSETVLI_E32(vl, 4)` (slideup vl). The bias vle32 needs e32 m1 — same SEW from prior vsetvli, OK.

Could we **hoist `NDS_VEC_VLW_V(V30, bias)` to BEFORE the slideup chain** so it loads in parallel with the vslideups (VLSU + VPERMUT, different FUs)? Let me check uarch — VLSU + VPERMUT: VLSU read ports 4,5; VPERMUT read ports 9,10. No overlap. VLSU write port 0; VPERMUT write port 3. No overlap. **CLEAN dual-issue pair!**

Currently:
```
vsetvli vl=4 e32 m1     ; line 491
12 vslideup             ; lines 492-506 (12 cyc on VPERMUT)
vle32 V30, bias         ; line 509 (would take ~16 cyc latency D$)
4 vadd                  ; lines 513-516 (consumer of V30; stalls until vle32 done)
```

If we hoist:
```
vsetvli vl=4 e32 m1
vle32 V30, bias         ; <-- HOIST HERE; runs in parallel with slideups
12 vslideup
4 vadd                  ; V30 ready by now (12 cyc + extra)
```

Savings: vle32 latency hidden. The vle32 at e32 m1 vl=4 is partial-VL → throughput = vector_length = 4 cyc throughput. Latency = 4 + L = 16 cyc (if D$ hit). Currently vle32 issues, then 4 vadd waits — 4 vadds are on VALU, vadd takes 2 cyc lat. So vle32→vadd RAW waits ~16 cyc. After hoist: vle32 issues, 12 vslideup take ~12 cyc on VPERMUT, then vadd waits = 16-12 = 4 cyc. Savings ~12 cyc/cur_row × 8 cur_rows × 100 calls = **9600 cyc = 1.7%**.

Actually this is the SAME win the s8 sibling got in r1-r4 ("hoist vle32 above slideup chain"). The u8 version has only 1 vle (bias), not 3 (no out_shift/out_scale arrays since they're scalar). Smaller win but real.

Lines changed: ~3 (move 2 lines up). Risk: very low. This is **textbook hoist pattern**.

Let me also verify whether the slideup vsetvli changes vl from col_src1 to 4 — yes line 491 sets vl=4. The bias vle32 at vl=4 e32 m1 is correct (need 4 elements). After hoist, the bias load happens with vl=4. Good.

Wait — but actually there's a subtle issue. Looking at line 510: `bias += vl`. After the hoist, `vl` is still 4 from the vsetvli at line 491, so `bias += 4` = correct.

Wait — actually the current code has the bias vle BEFORE the bias += vl bump. With the `bias += vl` happening at line 510 then `bias` register isn't advanced until after the vle. After hoist, the order would be:
```
vle32 V30, bias        ; uses current bias
12 vslideup            
4 vadd
... 
bias += vl;            ; needs to still be after vle to be correct
```

Yes, bias bump must be after vle32. Easy.

This is **H1 - high confidence** with concrete uarch backing.

Now let me consider 1 more idea — **(a) Conv-side wins**. Looking at the conv-side code: pOut3 and pOut4 (line 385-386) are computed once outside the rowCnt loop as `out + out_tensor_ch` and `out + out_tensor_ch * 2`, but `out` is the unroll4's local arg = `pOut` advanced each call. Actually they're computed per-call. Per cur_row, the 4 output streams are at out, out+OUT_CH, out+2*OUT_CH, out+3*OUT_CH. The function-call overhead per call: 5 calls × ~12 saves/restores = ~60 instrs/call × 100 = 6000 instrs. Small.

OK I have enough. Let me write the report.

# Analyze round 2: conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast (post-revert)

## Round 1 lesson reinforced
Round 1 wholesale SW-pipeline rewrite duplicated work GCC's auto-scheduler is already doing. Looking at the post-revert perf objdump (0x11328-0x11424), GCC has already emitted an alternating-buffer SW-pipeline (Phase A loads V24-V31 + computes V16-V23, Phase B loads V16-V23 + computes V24-V31), so any manual SW-pipe rewrite is pure scaffolding overhead. The "K=288" estimate in the round-1 lesson was actually wrong (perf-test is IN_CH=128, KER_DIM=3 -> K=1152, ~9 inner iters per cur_row), but the conclusion is right for a different reason: the compiler already pipelined.

## Wiki insights for low-risk peepholes
- **VLSU + VPERMUT dual-issue is clean** (uarch spec, ax45mpv-vpu-uarch-spec.md): VLSU read ports 4-5 vs VPERMUT 9-10; VLSU write port 0 vs VPERMUT write port 3. Zero conflict. Hoisting `vle32 bias` above `vslideup` chain runs them in parallel.
- **vmac + VLSU also clean** (ax45mpv-v1024-8mb-pipeline-tuning.md): VLSU as 2nd slot is allowed; this is what GCC already exploits in the inner-loop SW-pipeline (8 vle8 overlap with 16 vd4dotsu via different FUs).
- **VTYPE switches between e32 m1 epilogue ops are mandatory** -- no merge opportunity around vnsra/vnclipu narrowing chain.

content.text: Round 2: 576115 -> 576053 (-62 cyc, neutral within noise). Same instr count - confirms hoist landed but bias was already cached. Keeping change and trying H3 (interleave vadd with vslideup).

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash << 'OUTER'
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" <<'PYEOF'
import json,sys
from datetime import datetime
f=sys.argv[1]
s=json.load(open(f))
s["cycles_after"]="576053"
s["instrs_after"]="392760"
s["speedup"]="1.0001"
hist=s.get("round_history",[])
hist.append({
  "round":2,
  "approach":"Hoist NDS_VEC_VLW_V(V30,bias) above 12-vslideup chain (both unroll4 + tail kernel)",
  "cycles":"576053","instrs":"392760","ipc":"0.68","speedup":"1.0001",
  "outcome":"neutral",
  "insight":"Hoist landed (instr count unchanged) but bias load was likely L1-cached -- no measurable latency to hide. Saved 62 cyc (~noise). Pattern still worth keeping."
})
s["round_history"]=hist
# commit this round
import subprocess
prev_step=s.get("step","measure")
now=datetime.now()
s["step"]="learn"
s["history"]=s.get("history",[])+[f"{now.isoformat()} learn (commit r2)"]
s["prev_step_started"]=now.isoformat()
s["step_started"]=now.isoformat()

git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" commit -m "$(cat <<'COMMIT'
[opt/rvv] conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast: r2 hoist bias vle32 above slideup chain (neutral)

nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c: hoist NDS_VEC_VLW_V(V30, bias) from after the
12-vslideup chain to immediately after the VSETVLI_E32(vl, 4) preamble, in BOTH
the unroll4 hot kernel and the M2 tail kernel (2 sites).

Mechanism: VLSU + VPERMUT dual-issue clean (no VRF port conflict); the vle32
runs in parallel with the slideup chain on a different FU.

FPGA: 576115 -> 576053 cyc (-62 cyc, 1.0001x). Instruction count unchanged.
Saved cycles within FPGA noise -- bias load was likely L1-cached so there was
no latency to hide. Keeping for the pattern (no instr cost, mirror s8 commit
be791370 r1-r4 which had multiple vle32 to hoist).

Test: t_conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast 3/3 sub-tests pass on FPGA-measured
binary.

Co-Authored-By: optimize-skill <noreply@anthropic.com>
COMMIT
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
OUTER

23	#include "riscv_nn_support.h"
24	
25	//// Convolution Functions
26	int32_t riscv_nn_conv_HWC_u8_u8_s8_sym_bias_fast(const u8_t * in_tensor,
27	        const uint16_t in_tensor_dim,
28	        const uint16_t in_tensor_ch,
29	        const q7_t * ker_weight,
30	        const uint16_t out_tensor_ch,
31	        const uint16_t ker_dim,
32	        const uint16_t pad,
33	        const uint16_t stride,
34	        const q31_t * bias,
35	        const uint16_t pre_rshift,
36	        const uint16_t out_scale,
37	        const uint16_t post_rshift,
38	        u8_t * out_tensor,
39	        const uint16_t out_tensor_dim,
40	        q15_t * in_tmp_buf)
41	{
42	#ifdef ENA_EMPTY_FUN
43	    //empty body
44	#else
45	    if (in_tensor_ch % 4 != 0 || out_tensor_ch % 2 != 0)
46	    {
47	        /* check if the input dimension meets the constraints */
48	        return -1;
49	    }
50	
51	#if defined(ENA_VEC_ISA) && defined(ENA_TILING)
52	    int16_t   i_out_y, i_out_x, i_ker_y, i_ker_x;
53	
54	    /*
55	     *  Use im2col with 4-column batching and nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft_unroll4
56	     *  which implements a 4x4 tiled GEMM with vd4dotsu, vectorizing across the
57	     *  reduction dimension K (=in_tensor_ch * ker_dim * ker_dim). This achieves
58	     *  full vector utilization even when out_tensor_ch is small.
59	     */
60

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.subject: Optimization rounds 2-10 (alternative uarch tuning)
content.description: Try non-SW-pipeline approaches: peepholes, vsetvli barrier reduction, V31 hoist, requantize chain reorder

content.text: Round 3 regressed +1600 cyc. Reverting.

input.command:
/usr/bin/bash << 'OUTER'
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
REF=$(python3 -c "import json;print(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}'))['checkpoint_ref'])")
git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" checkout "${REF}" -- Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c
/usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh" Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mul_kernel_u8_bias_2sft.c 2>&1 | tail -3
python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" <<'PYEOF'
import json,sys
from datetime import datetime
f=sys.argv[1]
s=json.load(open(f))
hist=s.get("round_history",[])
hist.append({
  "round":3,"approach":"Interleave 4 vadd into 12-vslideup chain (after each 3 slideups)",
  "cycles":"577653","instrs":"392760","ipc":"0.68","speedup":"0.9972",
  "outcome":"regressed",
  "insight":"vadd dispatched at slideup window but stalled waiting for V30 (vle32 latency). Net +1600 cyc (~0.28%). VALU+VPERMUT dual-issue exists but bias-RAW dependency negates it."
})
s["round_history"]=hist
s["cycles_after"]="576053"
s["round"]=4
s["history"]=s.get("history",[])+[f"{datetime.now().isoformat()} reverted r3, advancing to r4"]
json.dump(s,open(f,"w"),indent=2)
PYEOF
OUTER
